problem.title: Design a URL Shortener
problem.brief: Design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Users submit long URLs and get back a short slug; anyone can later visit the slug and be redirected to the original URL.
problem.opening_prompt: Here's the problem: design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Users submit long URLs and get back a short slug; anyone who visits that slug later gets redirected to the original URL. Take it from there — let's start with how you'd frame the requirements.

sections.label: Requirements Clarification
sections.goal: Bound scope and establish non-functional targets before touching architecture
sections.objectives: Explicit in/out scope list; read/write ratio and rough QPS; p99 latency and availability targets named
good_signals: Asks about read/write ratio before proposing anything
Identifies p99 redirect latency as the dominant SLO unprompted
weak_signals: Jumps to architecture without establishing NFRs
Treats availability and latency as afterthoughts
sections.faang_bar: Senior IC drives the requirements themselves, quantifies redirect QPS at hundreds of thousands per second, names cold-link long-tail as a constraint, and ties NFRs to architectural decisions before drawing anything.

signals.description: Asked clarifying questions before touching architecture
Identified latency, availability, consistency, durability needs
Named read/write asymmetry as a structural constraint

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Bounds scope unprompted with in/out lists. Names 1-2 concrete NFRs. Asks about scale boundaries. Identifies custom slug, analytics, expiry. Names a 100:1 read/write ratio and p99 redirect latency as the SLO.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Drives the requirements list themselves. Quantifies redirect QPS at hundreds of thousands per second unprompted. Names cold-link long-tail as a constraint. Ties every NFR to an architectural implication.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Surfaces abuse risk and link rotation policy unprompted in requirements. Questions assumptions in the problem statement. Frames SLO in cost-per-redirect, not just latency. Names explicit out-of-scope items and explains why.

sections.label: High Level Architecture
sections.goal: Separate write API from redirect read tier; identify the dominant read path
sections.objectives: Write path and redirect path separated; caching layer named and positioned correctly; database choice justified against read load
good_signals: Separates write API from redirect service structurally
Places cache in front of DB on read path
Justifies DB choice against read/write ratio
weak_signals: Single monolithic service for reads and writes
No mention of caching on the redirect path
sections.faang_bar: Candidate names separate write and redirect services, puts a CDN or in-process cache on the redirect path, and justifies their storage choice against the 100:1 read/write ratio.

signals.description: Separate services or at least separate paths for write vs redirect
Cache positioned correctly on the redirect hot path
Database or KV store choice justified against workload characteristics

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Draws write API and redirect service separately. Names a cache. Picks a storage layer but doesn't deeply justify it.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Separates write and redirect services with clear rationale. Designs cache with TTL reasoning tied to the link expiry requirement. Justifies storage choice against both write rate and redirect latency SLO.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Reasons about CDN edge caching for the redirect path unprompted. Identifies the cold-start problem for new links. Proposes async write path for click counting to avoid write amplification on the hot path.

sections.label: Deep Dive — ID Generation & Redirect Path
sections.goal: Concrete ID allocation strategy that scales horizontally without coordination
sections.objectives: ID generation strategy named and collision handling explained; redirect path latency characterized; horizontal scaling approach described
good_signals: Proposes a concrete ID generation strategy (hash, counter, random + retry, Snowflake-style)
Reasons about redirect path latency end-to-end
weak_signals: Vague on ID generation ('just use a hash')
No latency reasoning on the redirect path
sections.faang_bar: Candidate proposes a concrete ID allocator (not just 'hash the URL'), explicitly addresses custom slug collision with first-write-wins semantics, and traces the redirect path end-to-end with latency reasoning at each hop.

signals.description: Concrete ID generation approach with reasoning
Custom slug collision handled with first-write-wins or equivalent
Redirect path traced end-to-end with latency characterization
ID generation scales horizontally without a coordination bottleneck

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Names an ID generation approach. Addresses collision at a high level. Describes redirect path at component level.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Proposes a horizontally scalable ID allocator with concrete collision probability reasoning. Handles custom slug collision with first-write-wins semantics. Traces redirect path with per-hop latency estimates.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Identifies the birthday paradox implications for short slug collision probability and sizes the slug length accordingly. Proposes ID strategy that doesn't require global coordination. Reasons about the long-tail cold-link problem and its impact on cache hit rate.

sections.label: Tradeoffs & Alternatives
sections.goal: Name and reason about at least 2 explicit design tradeoffs
sections.objectives: Two or more tradeoffs named with both sides articulated; alternatives proposed not just listed
good_signals: Names a tradeoff with both sides explicitly stated
Compares two concrete alternatives, not just mentions them
Connects tradeoff to the specific constraints of this problem
weak_signals: One-sided tradeoff ('X is better because Y')
Abstract tradeoffs disconnected from problem constraints
sections.faang_bar: Candidate names at least two tradeoffs with both sides explicit and connects each to the specific scale/latency/cost constraints of this problem.

signals.description: Both sides of a tradeoff articulated, not just one side
Concrete alternatives compared rather than just listed
Tradeoffs connected to this problem's specific constraints

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Names 1-2 tradeoffs. States both sides. Doesn't deeply connect to problem constraints.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Names 2+ tradeoffs with both sides explicit. Connects each to the specific scale/latency/cost constraints. Proposes a concrete recommendation with justification.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Surfaces tradeoffs that aren't obvious — e.g. cost of strong consistency on slugs at global scale, or the ops burden of a distributed ID generator vs a centralized one. Frames tradeoffs in terms of team ownership and operational cost, not just technical properties.

sections.label: Reliability & Operations
sections.goal: Monitor redirect SLO; define failure behavior and recovery path
sections.objectives: SLO defined and metric named; monitoring approach described; failure behavior and fallback articulated
good_signals: Names a specific SLO metric (p99 redirect latency, availability %)
Proposes a concrete monitoring signal, not just 'use metrics'
Describes failure behavior and fallback explicitly
weak_signals: Vague on monitoring ('we'd add some dashboards')
sections.faang_bar: Candidate defines a concrete redirect SLO, names the specific signal they'd alert on, and describes degraded-mode behavior when a dependency fails.

signals.description: Concrete alerting signal described, not vague 'monitoring'
Failure behavior and fallback explicitly described

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Names an SLO. Mentions monitoring. Describes basic failure behavior.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Defines a concrete redirect SLO tied to the p99 latency target. Names the specific alert signal and threshold. Describes degraded-mode behavior when cache or DB fails, with explicit fallback.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Connects SLO to business impact. Defines error budget and burn rate alerting. Describes the on-call runbook structure and how they'd communicate a SLO breach to stakeholders. Thinks about capacity planning and traffic forecasting as part of operations.

fault_scenarios: Your primary database just went down mid-traffic spike. Walk me through what happens end-to-end in your system.
Cache miss rate just spiked to 80%. What degrades first, and what's your response?
A user reports their short link is redirecting to the wrong URL. How do you debug that in production?
Your slug generation service is returning collisions at 10x the expected rate. What's happening and how do you recover?
You've just been told a high-profile link is being used for a phishing campaign. What does your system do, and how fast?
raise_stakes_prompts: How would you explain the cost model for this system to your VP — specifically the cost per redirect?
You're now being asked to support this service in 5 geographic regions. What breaks in your current design?
Three other product teams are now building features on top of your API. How does your design change?
Your slug namespace is getting exhausted — you're at 80% capacity. How do you handle that operationally?
You need to achieve 99.999% availability on the redirect path. Walk me through what that means architecturally and operationally.
